Home.subtitle: Climate Emergency Management Platform
Home.description: Multi-hazard risk intelligence with real-time weather monitoring and personalised emergency guidance for citizens.

Landing.featuresSubtitle: Real-time risk intelligence for every corner of Spain
Landing.monitoringDesc: Live weather data across all 52 Spanish provinces, updated continuously from AEMET stations.
Landing.mlModelsDesc: Dedicated models for flood, wildfire, drought, heatwave, seismic, coldwave, and windstorm risk.
Landing.emergencyDesc: AI-powered personalised safety instructions when a hazard threatens your area.
Landing.communityDesc: Citizen hazard reporting combined with official AEMET warnings for ground-truth awareness.

Predictions.title: Risk Predictions
Predictions.subtitle: Multi-model analysis for {province}
Predictions.howItWorksSummary: TrueRisk combines multiple machine learning models with real-time weather data, satellite observations, and historical disaster records to assess natural disaster risk across all 52 Spanish provinces.
Predictions.gumbelDesc: Extreme value analysis to estimate probability of rare events
Predictions.regressionDesc: 6h and 12h trend projection based on recent observations
Predictions.bayesianDesc: Real-time risk classification using Bayes' theorem
Predictions.emaDesc: Smoothing to reveal underlying trend direction
Predictions.zscoreDesc: Detection of unusual conditions by comparing to historical averages
Predictions.decisionTreeDesc: Emergency classification based on weather thresholds
Predictions.knnDesc: Matching with similar historical weather patterns
Predictions.limitationsText: Models are trained on historical data and may not capture unprecedented events. Predictions should complement, not replace, official AEMET warnings.
Predictions.headerSubtitle: ML model forecasts and extreme value analysis
Predictions.noForecastData: No forecast data available. TFT models have not been trained yet.
Predictions.forecastMethod: Temporal Fusion Transformers produce quantile forecasts (p10/p50/p90) at 6 horizons from +6h to +7d. A Graph Attention Network refines predictions using inter-province spatial context.
Predictions.forecastMethodFull: Temporal Fusion Transformers produce quantile forecasts (p10/p50/p90) at 6 horizons from +6h to +7d. A Graph Attention Network refines predictions using inter-province spatial context. Shaded bands show uncertainty range.
Predictions.tftAttentionSubtitle: Variable selection importance from Temporal Fusion Transformer
Predictions.tftAttentionMethod: The TFT attention mechanism learns which input features matter most for each prediction. Higher weights indicate stronger influence on the forecast.
Predictions.hydroMethod: Rational method (Q = C·I·A / 3.6) with time-of-concentration delay. Converts upstream precipitation forecasts to estimated downstream flow.
Predictions.explainerSummary: TrueRisk fuses real-time AEMET weather data, satellite imagery, and seismic catalogs through a multi-model machine-learning pipeline. Each hazard type runs its own trained classifier while statistical methods cross-validate the outputs to produce calibrated risk probabilities.

HazardModels.overviewSubtitle: Composite risk from all ML models
HazardModels.overviewMethod: Aggregates predictions from all seven hazard-specific models into a single composite risk score. The dominant hazard is identified as the highest individual score.
HazardModels.floodMethod: Gradient-boosted decision tree trained on historical DANA events. Ingests 23 features including soil moisture, pressure trends, 6h/24h/48h accumulated precipitation, river basin risk indices, and Mediterranean coastal exposure.
HazardModels.wildfireMethod: Ensemble of Random Forest and LightGBM classifiers using the Canadian Fire Weather Index system (FFMC, DMC, DC, ISI, BUI, FWI). 20 features include consecutive dry days, soil moisture, UV index, and elevation.
HazardModels.droughtMethod: Two-stage model: first computes SPEI at 1-month and 3-month scales. Then an LSTM neural network predicts 30-day drought trajectory from 90-day sequences.
HazardModels.heatwaveMethod: XGBoost classifier trained on 18 features including Wet Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT), consecutive hot days/nights tracking, temperature anomalies, and 48h max temperature forecasts.
HazardModels.seismicMethod: Analyzes recent seismic activity from Spain's IGN catalog within 200km of the province. Considers earthquake magnitude, frequency, proximity, and depth.
HazardModels.coldwaveMethod: Tracks wind chill temperature, consecutive cold days (max <5C) and cold nights (min <0C), elevation, and inland exposure. Designed to detect Filomena-type events.
HazardModels.windstormMethod: Analyzes sustained wind speed, gust intensity, and barometric pressure dynamics. Rapid pressure drops signal approaching storm systems.

StatisticalModels.gumbelSubtitle: Probability density of extreme weather events
StatisticalModels.gumbelMethod: Fits extreme value distributions to historical weather data to estimate the probability of rare events.
StatisticalModels.gumbelAnnotation: Higher values are rarer. The shaded zone marks extreme events beyond the 95th percentile.
StatisticalModels.regressionMethod: Applies linear regression to recent weather observations to identify trends and project values 6 and 12 hours ahead.
StatisticalModels.emaMethod: Applies an exponential moving average to smooth out short-term fluctuations in precipitation data, revealing the underlying trend direction and rate of change.
StatisticalModels.bayesianMethod: Combines prior knowledge about disaster frequencies with current weather conditions to update risk probabilities in real-time using Bayes' theorem.
StatisticalModels.zscoreMethod: Compares current weather values to historical averages using Z-scores. Values beyond ±2 standard deviations are flagged as anomalies.
StatisticalModels.anomaliesDetected: {count, plural, =0 {No anomalies} =1 {1 anomaly detected} other {# anomalies detected}}
StatisticalModels.decisionTreeMethod: Evaluates current conditions against a decision tree of weather thresholds to classify the most likely emergency type and confidence level.
StatisticalModels.knnSubtitle: Nearest historical weather events by Euclidean distance
StatisticalModels.knnMethod: Finds the most similar historical weather patterns and shows what happened next, ranked by similarity distance.
StatisticalModels.gevSubtitle: Generalized Extreme Value analysis with return level estimation
StatisticalModels.gevMethod: Fits generalized extreme value distributions to historical weather data to estimate the probability of rare events. The shape parameter (ξ) determines tail behavior: ξ=0 is Gumbel, ξ>0 is Fréchet (heavy tail), ξ<0 is Weibull (bounded tail).
StatisticalModels.bayesianSubtitleAlt: Prior vs posterior probability per disaster type
StatisticalModels.bayesianAnnotation: Faded bars show prior beliefs; solid bars show updated posterior probabilities. The multiplier shows how much evidence shifted the estimate.
StatisticalModels.zscoreMethodFull: Compares current weather values to historical averages using Z-scores. Values beyond ±2 standard deviations are flagged as anomalies, indicating unusual conditions.
StatisticalModels.explainerGumbel: Extreme-value distribution for return-period estimation of rare meteorological events.
StatisticalModels.explainerRegression: Trend fitting over rolling windows to project short-term variable trajectories.
StatisticalModels.explainerBayesian: Prior-weighted likelihood scoring for multi-hazard risk categorization.
StatisticalModels.explainerEma: Exponential moving averages to filter noise and detect directional momentum.
StatisticalModels.explainerZscore: Standard-deviation gating to flag statistically unusual observations.
StatisticalModels.explainerDecisionTree: Rule-based branching on threshold conditions for interpretable risk flags.
StatisticalModels.explainerKnn: K-nearest neighbor search against historical events to find analogous scenarios.

FirstAid.floodSteps: Move immediately to higher ground and avoid basements or low-lying areas. Do not walk, swim, or drive through flood waters — even 15 cm can knock you down. Disconnect electrical appliances and gas if safe to do so. Avoid contact with flood water as it may be contaminated with sewage or chemicals. If trapped in a building, go to the highest floor and signal for help from a window. Keep emergency supplies (water, torch, first aid kit) accessible at all times. After waters recede, do not return home until authorities confirm it is safe.
FirstAid.wildfireSteps: Evacuate immediately following official evacuation routes — do not wait. Cover your nose and mouth with a damp cloth to filter smoke and particles. Close all windows, doors, and vents in your home before leaving to slow fire spread. Wear long sleeves, trousers, and sturdy shoes to protect skin from embers. If trapped, move to a cleared area, a body of water, or lie face-down in a ditch covering your body. Never attempt to outrun a wildfire uphill — fire travels faster uphill. After evacuation, check for embers or spot fires around your property before re-entering.
FirstAid.heatwaveSteps: Move the person to a cool, shaded place immediately and lay them down. Apply cold, wet cloths or ice packs to the neck, armpits, and groin to cool the core body temperature. Give small sips of cool water if the person is conscious — never force fluids on someone who is vomiting. Call 112 immediately if the person loses consciousness, has a temperature above 40 C, or stops sweating. Avoid alcohol, caffeine, and sugary drinks during extreme heat — drink water regularly. Stay indoors during peak heat hours (12:00-17:00) and never leave children or pets in parked vehicles. Check on elderly neighbours and vulnerable people who may live alone.
FirstAid.earthquakeSteps: Drop to your hands and knees, take cover under a sturdy desk or table, and hold on until shaking stops. Move away from windows, mirrors, heavy furniture, and anything that could fall. If in bed, stay there and protect your head with a pillow — do not run outside. If outdoors, move to an open area away from buildings, power lines, and trees. If driving, pull over safely away from bridges, overpasses, and structures. After the shaking stops, expect aftershocks and be prepared to drop, cover, and hold again. Check for gas leaks (smell, hissing sound) and shut off the supply if you suspect a leak — do not use flames.
FirstAid.droughtSteps: Conserve water strictly — take short showers, fix leaks, and reuse grey water for plants. Store drinking water in clean, sealed containers (minimum 3 litres per person per day). Avoid outdoor physical activity during the hottest parts of the day to prevent dehydration. Recognise dehydration signs: dark urine, dizziness, dry mouth, and rapid heartbeat — seek medical help if severe. Do not use outdoor fires or barbecues — drought conditions dramatically increase wildfire risk. Follow local water restrictions and report illegal water use to authorities. Keep an emergency water supply for at least 72 hours for your household.
FirstAid.coldwaveSteps: Stay indoors and keep your home heated to at least 18 C — hypothermia can occur even indoors. Wear multiple loose layers of clothing rather than one thick layer to trap body heat effectively. Recognise hypothermia signs: shivering, confusion, slurred speech, drowsiness — call 112 immediately. Protect extremities (fingers, toes, ears, nose) from frostbite — keep them dry and covered. If you must go outside, limit exposure time and tell someone your planned route and return time. Check on elderly neighbours and vulnerable people regularly — they are at highest risk. Keep emergency supplies including blankets, torches, non-perishable food, and a battery-powered radio.
FirstAid.windstormSteps: Stay indoors away from windows, skylights, and glass doors — flying debris is the main danger. Secure or bring inside loose outdoor items (furniture, bins, plant pots) before the storm arrives. If driving, pull over away from trees and power lines and stay in the vehicle with your seatbelt on. Do not walk or cycle during the storm — wind gusts above 90 km/h can knock an adult off their feet. If caught outdoors, take shelter in a sturdy building or lie flat in a ditch away from trees. After the storm, avoid downed power lines and report them to 112 — they may still be live. Check your roof, windows, and structure for damage before re-entering your home.

Walkthrough.welcomeDesc: Your multi-hazard risk intelligence platform for Spain. We'll give you a quick tour of everything available — it only takes a moment.
Walkthrough.navDesc: Access every feature from this bar: Dashboard, Map, Reports, Preparedness, Safety check-ins, Evacuation routes, Drought monitor, Predictions, Alerts, Emergency SOS, and Phrase book.
Walkthrough.provinceDesc: Choose any of the 52 Spanish provinces. All data on the page — risk scores, weather, alerts — updates instantly for your selection.
Walkthrough.riskDesc: Your composite risk score from 0 (safe) to 100 (critical), with a breakdown across 7 hazards: flood, wildfire, drought, heatwave, seismic, coldwave, and windstorm.
Walkthrough.weatherDesc: Real-time conditions from Open-Meteo updated every 60 seconds — temperature, wind, precipitation, and humidity at a glance.
Walkthrough.alertsDesc: Live AEMET weather alerts and community reports streamed in real time. Severity-coded so you know what needs attention now.
Walkthrough.quickActionsDesc: One-tap access to call 112 emergency services or jump to critical pages when every second counts.
Walkthrough.preparednessDesc: Track your emergency kit, plan status, and readiness score. Build your preparedness over time with guided checklists.
Walkthrough.featuresDesc: TrueRisk gives you a full suite of tools to stay safe. Here's what you can explore beyond the dashboard:
Walkthrough.featureMapDesc: Explore risk layers, fire hotspots, earthquakes, and weather stations across Spain.
Walkthrough.featureEmergencyDesc: One-tap 112 call, emergency contacts by province, and first aid guides.
Walkthrough.featurePredictionsDesc: AI-powered forecasts with model explainability for each hazard type.
Walkthrough.featureEvacuationDesc: Find shelters, hospitals, and safe points with real-time route planning.
Walkthrough.featureSafetyDesc: Check in with family members and request safety confirmations.
Walkthrough.featureReportDesc: Search any address for a detailed risk analysis and flood zone assessment.

cookieBanner.message: This site uses essential cookies for authentication and language preferences. No tracking or analytics cookies are used.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.intro: TrueRisk is an open-source community project committed to protecting your privacy. This policy explains what data we collect, why, and how we protect it.

dataController.title: Data Controller
dataController.content: TrueRisk is an open-source community project hosted on GitHub. As there is no formal legal entity behind the project, the project maintainers act as data controllers. For any data-related inquiries, please open an issue on our GitHub repository or contact us via the details at the bottom of this page.

dataWeCollect.title: Data We Collect
dataWeCollect.intro: We collect different categories of data depending on how you use the platform:

identity.title: Identity & Authentication
identity.content: Email address, nickname, display name, avatar URL, and authentication provider (Google, GitHub, or credentials). Your email is encrypted at rest.

location.title: Location Data
location.content: Home and work addresses (encrypted at rest), geographic coordinates, province preferences, and — if you grant permission — real-time GPS location for emergency features.

health.title: Health & Medical
health.content: Medical conditions (encrypted at rest), mobility level, age range, and whether you depend on power for medical equipment. This data is used solely to personalise emergency guidance.

emergency.title: Emergency Contacts
emergency.content: Emergency contact names and phone numbers (both encrypted at rest). Used for the family safety check-in feature.

household.title: Household Information
household.content: Household member details, pet information, and special needs. Used to generate personalised preparedness plans and emergency guidance.

building.title: Building Details
building.content: Construction year, building materials, number of stories, floor level, and building condition. Used to assess vulnerability to natural hazards.

economic.title: Economic Vulnerability
economic.content: Income bracket, insurance status, property value range, and emergency savings. Used to tailor preparedness recommendations to your situation.

safety.title: Safety Check-ins
safety.content: Your safety status during emergencies (safe, need help, evacuating, sheltering), optional location and messages. Shared with your linked family members.

family.title: Family Links
family.content: Connections to other users you designate as family, friends, or neighbours. Used for mutual safety check-ins during emergencies.

community.title: Community Reports
community.content: Hazard reports you submit including type, severity, location, description, and optional photos. These are visible to other users in the affected area.

preparedness.title: Preparedness Data
preparedness.content: Checklist completion status, emergency plans (including meeting points, communication plans, insurance information, and document locations), and preparedness scores over time.

notifications.title: Notification Preferences
notifications.content: Push notification endpoints, phone numbers for SMS alerts, Telegram chat IDs, and your alert delivery preferences (channels, severity thresholds, quiet hours).

legalBasis.title: Legal Basis for Processing
legalBasis.intro: Under GDPR Article 6, we process your data on the following bases:
legalBasis.consent: Consent — You choose to create an account and provide optional profile information. You can withdraw consent at any time by deleting your account.
legalBasis.contract: Contract performance — Processing necessary to provide the service you registered for (risk alerts, emergency guidance, preparedness tools).
legalBasis.legitimate: Legitimate interest — Security measures, abuse prevention, and service improvement.
legalBasis.vital: Vital interests — Emergency safety features (safety check-ins, family status) where processing may be necessary to protect life.

howWeUse.title: How We Use Your Data
howWeUse.content: Your data is used to: calculate personalised risk scores for your location, send relevant hazard alerts, generate emergency guidance tailored to your profile (health, mobility, household), enable family safety coordination during emergencies, track your preparedness progress, and facilitate community hazard reporting.

storage.title: Data Storage & Encryption
storage.content: Your data is stored on servers located in the European Union (Cubepath hosting infrastructure). Sensitive fields — including email addresses, home and work addresses, medical conditions, emergency contact names, and phone numbers — are encrypted at rest using Fernet symmetric encryption. Passwords are hashed and never stored in plain text.

retention.title: Data Retention
retention.content: Account data is retained for as long as your account exists. Property risk reports expire automatically after 30 days. Safety check-ins expire after their configured duration. You can delete your entire account and all associated data at any time through the profile settings.

thirdParty.title: Third-Party Services
thirdParty.intro: TrueRisk integrates with the following external services:
thirdParty.google: Google OAuth — Authentication provider. Subject to Google's Privacy Policy.
thirdParty.github: GitHub OAuth — Authentication provider. Subject to GitHub's Privacy Policy.
thirdParty.openmeteo: Open-Meteo — Open-source weather API. Receives province-level location data (not personal data) to fetch weather information.
thirdParty.aemet: AEMET (Spanish State Meteorological Agency) — Official weather and alert data. No personal data is shared.
thirdParty.maplibre: OpenFreeMap / MapLibre — Map tile rendering. Your browser requests map tiles directly; IP address is visible to the tile server.
thirdParty.nominatim: Nominatim / OpenStreetMap — Geocoding service. Addresses are sent to resolve coordinates. Subject to OpenStreetMap's privacy policy.

transfers.title: International Data Transfers
transfers.content: Your core data is stored within the EU. However, if you authenticate via Google or GitHub, those providers may process authentication data outside the EU under their own GDPR-compliant data processing agreements.

rights.title: Your Rights
rights.intro: Under GDPR, you have the following rights:
rights.access: Right of access — Request a copy of all data we hold about you.
rights.rectification: Right to rectification — Correct inaccurate data through your profile settings or by contacting us.
rights.erasure: Right to erasure — Delete your account and all associated data at any time via profile settings.
rights.portability: Right to data portability — Request your data in a structured, machine-readable format.
rights.restriction: Right to restriction — Request that we limit processing of your data.
rights.objection: Right to object — Object to processing based on legitimate interest.
rights.withdraw: Right to withdraw consent — Withdraw consent at any time without affecting prior processing.

exerciseRights.title: How to Exercise Your Rights
exerciseRights.content: To exercise any of these rights, you can: delete your account directly through profile settings, open an issue on our GitHub repository, or email us at the address listed below. We will respond within 30 days as required by GDPR.

children.title: Children's Privacy
children.content: TrueRisk is not directed at children under 16 years of age. We do not knowingly collect personal data from children under 16. If you believe a child has provided us with personal data, please contact us and we will delete it.

changes.title: Changes to This Policy
changes.content: We may update this privacy policy from time to time. Changes will be posted on this page with an updated date. Continued use of TrueRisk after changes constitutes acceptance of the updated policy.

contact.title: Contact
contact.content: For privacy-related inquiries, please open an issue on our GitHub repository or email us at:

terms.title: Terms of Use
terms.intro: By accessing or using TrueRisk, you agree to be bound by these terms. If you do not agree, please do not use the service.

acceptance.title: Acceptance of Terms
acceptance.content: These Terms of Use govern your access to and use of TrueRisk, a multi-hazard risk intelligence platform for Spain. By creating an account or using any features of the platform, you accept these terms in full.

serviceDescription.title: Description of Service
serviceDescription.content: TrueRisk provides ML-powered risk scoring across 7 natural hazards (flood, wildfire, drought, heatwave, seismic, cold wave, windstorm) for all 52 Spanish provinces. Features include real-time weather monitoring, personalised emergency guidance, community hazard reporting, family safety check-ins, and preparedness planning tools.

accounts.title: Account Registration
accounts.content: You must provide accurate information when creating an account. You are responsible for maintaining the security of your password and account. One account per person. You must be at least 16 years old to create an account.

acceptableUse.title: Acceptable Use
acceptableUse.content: You agree not to: submit false or misleading community hazard reports, use automated systems to scrape or overload the platform, impersonate other users or officials, use the platform for any unlawful purpose, or attempt to gain unauthorised access to other accounts or system infrastructure.

riskDisclaimer.title: Risk Information Disclaimer
riskDisclaimer.content: Risk scores, hazard predictions, and AI-generated summaries provided by TrueRisk are for informational purposes only. They are produced by machine learning models and may contain inaccuracies or errors.
riskDisclaimer.notSubstitute: TrueRisk is not a substitute for official emergency services. In any emergency, always follow the instructions of Protección Civil, AEMET, and emergency services (112). Do not rely solely on TrueRisk for life-safety decisions.
riskDisclaimer.noGuarantee: We make no guarantee that the platform will be available during actual emergency events. Network conditions, server capacity, and other factors may affect availability when you need it most.

communityContent.title: Community Reports & User Content
communityContent.content: Community hazard reports are submitted by users and are not verified by official sources. TrueRisk is not liable for the accuracy of user-submitted content. By submitting a report, you grant TrueRisk a non-exclusive license to display it to other users in the affected area. You retain ownership of your content.

intellectualProperty.title: Intellectual Property
intellectualProperty.content: TrueRisk is open-source software released under the MIT License. You are free to use, modify, and distribute the source code in accordance with the license terms. The TrueRisk name and logo are project trademarks used to identify this specific deployment.

liability.title: Limitation of Liability
liability.content: TrueRisk is provided as an open-source community project on an "as is" and "as available" basis, without warranties of any kind, express or implied. To the maximum extent permitted by law, the project contributors shall not be liable for any damages arising from: decisions made based on risk scores or predictions, inaccurate or delayed hazard information, service unavailability during emergencies, loss of data, or any other use of the platform.

availability.title: Service Availability
availability.content: TrueRisk is a community project with no Service Level Agreement (SLA). The platform may be unavailable for maintenance, updates, or due to infrastructure issues. We make best efforts to maintain uptime but provide no guarantees.

termination.title: Termination
termination.content: You may delete your account at any time through profile settings, which permanently removes all your data. We reserve the right to suspend or terminate accounts that violate these terms or engage in abusive behaviour.

governingLaw.title: Governing Law
governingLaw.content: These terms are governed by the laws of Spain. Any disputes shall be submitted to the jurisdiction of the courts of Spain.

changesToTerms.title: Changes to These Terms
changesToTerms.content: We may update these terms from time to time. Changes will be posted on this page with an updated date. Continued use of TrueRisk after changes constitutes acceptance of the updated terms.

cookies.title: Cookie Policy
cookies.intro: This policy explains how TrueRisk uses cookies and similar technologies.

whatAreCookies.title: What Are Cookies
whatAreCookies.content: Cookies are small text files stored on your device by your web browser. They are used to remember preferences and enable essential functionality. Local storage is a similar browser technology that stores data locally on your device.

cookiesWeUse.title: Cookies We Use
cookiesWeUse.intro: TrueRisk uses only essential and functional cookies:

locale.name: locale
locale.purpose: Stores your language preference (Spanish or English)

localStorage.title: Local Storage
localStorage.intro: TrueRisk also uses browser local storage for the following:

province.name: truerisk-province
province.purpose: Your app preferences including selected province, locale, map layer settings, and notification preferences

consent.name: truerisk-cookie-consent
consent.purpose: Remembers that you have seen and dismissed the cookie information banner

noTracking.title: No Analytics or Tracking
noTracking.content: TrueRisk does not use any analytics, advertising, or tracking cookies. We do not use Google Analytics, Facebook Pixel, or any similar tracking technology. Your browsing behaviour on TrueRisk is not tracked or profiled.

thirdPartyCookies.title: Third-Party Cookies
thirdPartyCookies.content: TrueRisk does not set any third-party cookies. When you authenticate via Google or GitHub, you are redirected to their sites which may set their own cookies. These are governed by their respective cookie policies.

managing.title: Managing Cookies
managing.content: You can control cookies through your browser settings. Most browsers allow you to block or delete cookies. However, disabling essential cookies will prevent you from logging in to TrueRisk. Clearing local storage will reset your app preferences.

changes.title: Changes to This Policy
changes.content: We may update this cookie policy from time to time. Changes will be posted on this page with an updated date.

license.title: License

whatThisMeans.title: What This Means
whatThisMeans.content: The MIT License is one of the most permissive open-source licenses. You are free to:
whatThisMeans.use: Use TrueRisk for any purpose, including commercial use
whatThisMeans.modify: Modify the source code to suit your needs
whatThisMeans.distribute: Distribute copies of the original or modified code
whatThisMeans.condition: The only condition is that you include the original copyright notice and license text in any copy or substantial portion of the software.

contributing.title: Contributing
contributing.content: We welcome contributions from the community. To contribute, visit our GitHub repository, fork the project, and submit a pull request. All contributions are made under the same MIT License.

thirdParty.title: Third-Party Licenses
thirdParty.intro: TrueRisk is built with the following open-source technologies:

mission.title: Our Mission
mission.content: TrueRisk aims to make natural hazard risk information accessible, understandable, and actionable for everyone in Spain. We believe that every citizen deserves real-time, personalised risk intelligence — not just raw data, but clear guidance on what to do when hazards threaten their area.

project.title: The Project
project.content: TrueRisk is an open-source community project hosted on GitHub under the MIT License. It was created to address the gap between available hazard data and citizen preparedness. The platform combines official data sources with machine learning to provide risk intelligence that was previously available only to specialised agencies.

whatWeDo.title: What We Do
whatWeDo.content: TrueRisk monitors 7 natural hazards across all 52 Spanish provinces: floods, wildfires, drought, heatwaves, seismic events, cold waves, and windstorms. Each hazard has a dedicated ML model (XGBoost, LightGBM, or PyTorch) that processes real-time weather data, historical patterns, and geographic features to produce risk scores updated continuously.

thirdParty.title: Data Sources & Attribution
thirdParty.intro: TrueRisk relies on the following external services and data sources:

openmeteo.name: Open-Meteo
openmeteo.description: Open-source weather API providing real-time and forecast data

aemet.name: AEMET
aemet.description: Spanish State Meteorological Agency — official weather observations, forecasts, and hazard warnings

openfreemap.name: OpenFreeMap / MapLibre
openfreemap.description: Open-source map tile rendering and interactive map components

nominatim.name: Nominatim / OpenStreetMap
nominatim.description: Geocoding service for resolving addresses to coordinates

ine.name: INE
ine.description: Spanish National Statistics Institute — province and municipality demographic data

techStack.title: Technology Stack
techStack.frontend: Frontend: Next.js 16, React 19, Tailwind CSS v4, MapLibre GL, Framer Motion, Recharts
techStack.ml: ML/AI: XGBoost, LightGBM, PyTorch (Temporal Fusion Transformer)

contribute.title: How to Contribute
contribute.content: TrueRisk is open to contributions of all kinds: code, documentation, translations, bug reports, and feature suggestions. Visit our GitHub repository to get started.

contact.title: Contact
contact.content: For general inquiries, feature requests, or bug reports, please open an issue on our GitHub repository. For privacy-related matters, see our Privacy Policy.

commitment.title: Our Commitment
commitment.content: TrueRisk is committed to ensuring digital accessibility for all users, including people with disabilities. We strive to conform to the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1 at Level AA.

features.title: Accessibility Features
features.semantic: Semantic HTML structure throughout the application
features.keyboard: Keyboard navigation support for all interactive elements
features.aria: ARIA labels and roles for dynamic content
features.contrast: Colour contrast ratios meeting WCAG AA standards for text
features.responsive: Responsive design that works at various zoom levels (up to 200%)
features.motion: Reduced motion support — animations respect prefers-reduced-motion
features.focus: Visible focus indicators on all interactive elements

limitations.title: Known Limitations
limitations.map: Interactive map (MapLibre GL) — The map component has limited keyboard and screen reader support. Province selection and hazard data are also available through non-map interfaces.
limitations.charts: Data visualisations (Recharts) — Charts provide visual representation of risk data. Key data points are available in text form elsewhere in the interface.
limitations.realtime: Real-time updates — Some dynamic content (SSE alerts, live weather) may not immediately announce to screen readers.

assistive.title: Assistive Technology
assistive.content: TrueRisk has been developed with compatibility for screen readers, keyboard-only navigation, and browser zoom. We test with common assistive technologies and welcome feedback on accessibility issues.

reporting.title: Reporting Issues
reporting.content: If you encounter any accessibility barriers while using TrueRisk, please let us know. Accessibility issues are treated as high-priority bugs. You can report issues on our GitHub repository or contact us directly.

Chat.title: AI Assistant
Chat.emptyState: Ask me anything — weather, risks, emergencies, or any question you have.
Chat.conversationHint: Messages in this conversation are limited
Chat.error_injection_detected: Your message was flagged for safety reasons.
Chat.error_off_topic: I can only help with risk and emergency topics.
Chat.error_daily_limit: Daily message limit reached. Try again tomorrow.
Chat.error_cooldown: Please wait a few seconds between messages.
Chat.error_conversation_limit: Conversation limit reached. Start a new chat.
Chat.error_stream_error: Something went wrong. Please try again.
Chat.errorGeneric: Something went wrong. Please try again.

Docs.title: API Documentation
Docs.subtitle: REST API for multi-hazard risk intelligence across all 52 Spanish provinces. Real-time risk scoring powered by 7 ML models, live weather data from AEMET and Open-Meteo, and community-sourced hazard reports.
Docs.quickStartDesc: No authentication required for public read endpoints. Fetch risk scores for any province in seconds.
Docs.authDesc: Public endpoints (risk scores, weather, provinces) require no authentication. Write operations and user-specific data require a JWT token obtained via /auth/login or /auth/register. Include the token in the Authorization header.
Docs.endpointsDesc: All endpoints are prefixed with /api. Parameters in braces are path variables (e.g. province_code is the 2-digit INE code like 28 for Madrid).

risk.name: Risk & Analysis
risk.desc: Real-time composite risk scores, forecasts, AI-generated summaries, and model explainability for all provinces.

weather.name: Weather & Environment
weather.desc: Live data from multiple sources: earthquakes, wildfires, air quality, reservoirs, river gauges, drought, and flash flood monitoring.

geography.name: Geography & Climate
geography.desc: Province and municipality data, climate projections, and location services for reverse geocoding.

alerts.name: Alerts & Safety
alerts.desc: Active alerts, real-time AEMET warnings via SSE streaming, emergency planning, evacuation routes, and safety tips.

community.name: Community & AI
community.desc: Citizen hazard reports with upvoting and verification, AI chat assistant, and location-based suggestions.

data.name: Data Sources
data.desc: Demographic, vegetation, seismic, energy, and weather station data for detailed environmental analysis.

notifications.name: Notifications
notifications.desc: Push notification subscriptions, SMS and email alerts, and Telegram bot integration.

auth.name: Auth & User
auth.desc: User registration, JWT authentication, profile management, property risk reports, and insurance estimates.

Docs.rateLimitDesc: API requests are rate-limited to ensure fair usage. Limits are applied per IP address for public endpoints and per user for authenticated endpoints.
Docs.errorsDesc: All errors follow a consistent JSON format with an error type, human-readable detail, HTTP status code, and ISO 8601 timestamp.
Docs.interactiveDesc: Try out endpoints directly in the browser with the auto-generated Swagger UI documentation.
